name: Sierra de la Culebra and Peña Mira Loop
name_original: Terruca y Sierra Culebra — circular por Mahide
description: Challenge yourself on this difficult 44.8-mile road cycling route through Sierra de la Culebra, featuring significant climbs and diverse lan
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Figueruela De Arriba traverse a varied landscape at the foot of the Sierra de la Culebra, offering diverse terrains for cyclists. The region features dramatic mountain scenery, verdant valleys like the Valle del Arroyo de la Fragua, and areas shaped by the Cabrón and Manzanas rivers. These natural features provide a mix of challenging climbs and scenic stretches, with routes extending towards the Portuguese border and Montesinho Natural Park. Best road cycling routes around Figueruela De Arriba The most popular road cycling route is Embalse de Cernadilla y Sierra de la Culebra — circular por Puebla de Sanabria, a 49.4 miles (79.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 58 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers extensive views of the Sierra de la Culebra. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sierra de la Culebra Loop from Mahide, a moderate 38.8 miles (62.5 km) path. This loop provides a comprehensive exploration of the Sierra de la Culebra's natural beauty. Local road cyclists also love the Sierra de la Culebra and Peña Mira Loop, a 44.8 miles (72.1 km) trail leading through the Sierra de la Culebra and past Peña Mira, often completed in about 3 hours 37 minutes. Road cycling around Figueruela De Arriba is defined by mountain ranges, river valleys, and diverse topography. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes with significant elevation changes. The routes in Figueruela De Arriba are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.25 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Figueruela De Arriba's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Figueruela de Arriba?
A: Road cycling routes around Figueruela de Arriba traverse a varied landscape, featuring dramatic mountain scenery from the Sierra de la Culebra and verdant valleys like the Valle del Arroyo de la Fragua. You'll encounter a mix of challenging climbs and scenic stretches, with routes extending towards the Portuguese border and Montesinho Natural Park, offering diverse topography.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for different fitness levels?
A: Yes, the region offers a range of road cycling experiences for various abilities. While there are no easy routes listed, you can find 12 moderate routes and 6 difficult routes. This means cyclists can find options tailored to their skill and endurance levels, from comprehensive explorations to more challenging rides with significant elevation changes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling in the area?
A: The routes offer stunning views of the Sierra de la Culebra, known for its abundant wildlife. You might also encounter the picturesque Valle del Arroyo de la Fragua and areas shaped by the Cabrón and Manzanas rivers. Notable highlights in the vicinity include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5437911" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Mira Summit&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5814010" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Weir and Boardwalk of the Rio Onor&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5422832" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fidionda Spring&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025465454/sierra-de-la-culebra-loop-from-mahide" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de la Culebra Loop from Mahide&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 38.8-mile path that provides a comprehensive exploration of the region's natural beauty. Another option is the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368702614/sierra-de-la-culebra-and-pena-mira-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de la Culebra and Peña Mira Loop&lt;/a&gt;, covering 44.8 miles.

Q: When is the best time of year to go road cycling in Figueruela de Arriba?
A: Given the region's dramatic mountain scenery and verdant valleys, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures and beautiful landscapes. Summer can be warm, especially in the valleys, while winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially challenging conditions in higher elevations of the Sierra de la Culebra.

Q: What is the longest road cycling route around Figueruela de Arriba?
A: One of the longer and more challenging routes is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368839991/embalse-de-cernadilla-y-sierra-de-la-culebra-circular-por-puebla-de-sanabria" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Cernadilla y Sierra de la Culebra — circular por Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 49.4 miles (79.5 km) and typically takes around 3 hours 58 minutes to complete, offering extensive views of the Sierra de la Culebra.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that cross into Portugal or near the border?
A: Figueruela de Arriba's proximity to the Portuguese border and the Montesinho Natural Park suggests potential for cross-border cycling adventures. While specific routes crossing the border are not detailed, the region's historical Roman road connections indicate pathways through varied terrain that extend towards Portugal, offering diverse landscapes.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Figueruela de Arriba?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.25 stars from over 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the dramatic mountain scenery, and the comprehensive exploration of natural beauty offered by routes like the Sierra de la Culebra loops.

Q: Are there any less crowded road cycling routes in the area?
A: While specific crowd levels are not tracked, the region is known for its natural beauty and diverse topography, providing ample space. Exploring routes that venture deeper into the Sierra de la Culebra or the Valle del Arroyo de la Fragua, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368828357/from-trabazos-to-castillo-de-outeiro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Trabazos to Castillo de Outeiro&lt;/a&gt;, might offer a more secluded experience away from main thoroughfares.

Q: Can I find viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in scenic vistas. The Sierra de la Culebra offers extensive views, and the Valle del Arroyo de la Fragua is highlighted for its spectacular views. You can also find specific viewpoints such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1956537" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of São Julião in the Montesinho Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, which can be incorporated into your rides.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes starting from Trabazos?
A: Yes, there are routes that start from or pass through Trabazos. For instance, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025525557/trabazos-tour-around-figueruela-de-arriba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trabazos – tour around Figueruela de Arriba&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 31.3-mile (50.4 km) loop, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368828357/from-trabazos-to-castillo-de-outeiro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Trabazos to Castillo de Outeiro&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 47.5-mile (76.5 km) route.

Q: What is the total number of road cycling routes available in the Figueruela de Arriba area?
A: There are 18 road cycling routes available around Figueruela de Arriba, offering a variety of distances and difficulty levels to explore the region's diverse landscapes.
tips: A small town in the Aliste region, Riomanzanas is located a few kilometers from the Sierra de la Culebra. The town is close to the border with Portugal.
Quiet road to the border between Portugal and Spain along the Guadramil river.

Ascent by bicycle to the El Portillón pass in the Sierra de la Culebra. From the top 1068m the views are spectacular. At 7.22km long and with an average gradient of 5.8%, it's not an easy climb. Go at your pace.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Terruca y Sierra Culebra circular route by racebike?
faqs.answer: This challenging route is designed for racebikes and generally takes around 3 hours and 37 minutes to complete. However, your actual duration may vary based on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route features a constantly undulating landscape with frequent ascents and descents, which will certainly test your legs. While much of it boasts perfect asphalt for enjoyable riding, be prepared for some varied surfaces. One section includes a strong climb on a forest track that was once an old road and can be a bit tricky for a road bike due to its poor condition.

faqs.question: Is the Terruca y Sierra Culebra circular route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is officially categorized as a 'difficult road route' and is not recommended for beginners. It requires good physical fitness due to significant elevation changes and varied road surfaces, including challenging climbs.

faqs.question: Where does the route start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and ends in the village of Mahide. While specific parking details aren't provided, Mahide is the primary access point, and you should be able to find suitable parking within the village.

faqs.question: What wildlife can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de la Culebra is renowned for its rich fauna. You may encounter roe deer, deer, and wild boar. Most notably, it is a significant habitat for the Iberian wolf, making it one of the best locations on the Iberian Peninsula for sighting these animals. The rutting season, typically mid-September to early October, is a particularly interesting time for wildlife observation.

faqs.question: What are some notable landmarks or scenic spots along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers extensive natural beauty and passes through picturesque villages. You'll encounter the Stone Bridge of Riomanzanas, the Border Crossing Between Spain and Portugal at the Guadramil River, and the challenging Puerto de Peña Mira Ascent, Sierra de la Culebra. The route also passes through charming villages like Santa Cruz de los Cuérragos, which is highlighted as a must-visit.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de la Culebra offers a unique experience throughout the year. For wildlife enthusiasts, mid-September to early October is ideal for observing the Iberian wolf during its rutting season. Generally, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the extremes of summer heat or winter cold.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle in Sierra de la Culebra?
faqs.answer: As a natural area, there are typically no specific permits or entrance fees required for general cycling activities within the Sierra de la Culebra. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning wildlife protection and private property.

faqs.question: Is the Terruca y Sierra Culebra route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route traverses natural areas, it is primarily a road cycling route. Dogs are generally allowed in public spaces and natural parks in Spain, but it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially given the presence of significant wildlife like the Iberian wolf. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and doesn't disturb the local fauna.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails. You'll cycle along parts of the CIMA ZM04 Peña Mira, Pista de Linarejos a San Pedro, Camino de Pedroso, Camino de La Cantera, Camino de debajo la Aldea, Cañada Real de ganados de Zamora a Sanabria, Camino de La Estación, Camino de La Urrieta, Camino de La Venta, Camino de Peña Mira, Camino nuevo a Peña Mira, Camino de Villarino, and Camino de Robledo.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several picturesque villages such as Flechas, Figueruela de Arriba, Villarino, Santa Cruz de los Cuérragos, Linarejos, and Villardeciervos, in addition to starting and ending in Mahide. These villages often offer local amenities like cafes, pubs, and potentially accommodation options for cyclists.
